topic: news story about a wrongful conviction — venting

turns.content: Have you been following the Andrew Malkinson situation? I know the original quashing was back in 2023 but the inquiry report has just come out and I am struggling to contain my anger.

I'm aware of the Andrew Malkinson case — he was wrongfully convicted of rape and spent 17 years in prison before his conviction was quashed. What specifically in the inquiry report has prompted this reaction?

The sheer catalogue of failures. The Greater Manchester Police failures with DNA evidence, the CCRC dragging its feet for years, the refusal to refer the case back to the Court of Appeal despite credible evidence of innocence. And now the inquiry lays it all out in meticulous detail and the response from the institutions involved is the usual performative contrition. 'Lessons will be learned.' That phrase should be struck from the English language.

The gap between institutional acknowledgment and meaningful reform is genuinely frustrating, and you're right that 'lessons will be learned' has become almost meaningless through overuse. In Malkinson's case, the timeline is particularly damning — the evidence that should have prevented or overturned the conviction existed for years before anyone acted on it. What aspect concerns you most as a practitioner? The police disclosure failures, the CCRC's role, or something else?

Disclosure. It is always disclosure. The police had DNA evidence from the crime scene that didn't match Malkinson. They had it. And it wasn't properly disclosed to the defence. This is not a novel problem — disclosure failures have been at the heart of virtually every major miscarriage of justice in this country for decades. Judith Ward. The Birmingham Six. The Guildford Four. And here we are in the twenty-first century and the same fundamental failing is still destroying lives.

You're identifying a systemic pattern that spans decades. The fact that disclosure failures keep recurring despite being central to some of the most high-profile miscarriages of justice in British legal history suggests the problem isn't about individual errors — it's structural. Do you think the issue is primarily one of resources, culture within police forces and the CPS, or something about the legal framework itself?

All three, but culture is the root of it. There is a mindset within certain parts of the police and prosecution that disclosure is an obstacle to conviction rather than a safeguard for justice. They see it as a bureaucratic burden. And when the pressure is on to get results, to close cases, to maintain conviction rates, the temptation to under-disclose or to fail to look for exculpatory material in the first place becomes overwhelming. The legal framework is adequate on paper. The Criminal Procedure and Investigations Act sets out clear obligations. But a framework is only as good as the people operating within it, and if the institutional culture treats disclosure as an afterthought, no amount of statutory language will fix it.

That's a sharp diagnosis. The distinction between the framework on paper and the culture in practice is crucial. If disclosure obligations are seen as a box-ticking exercise rather than a fundamental duty, then compliance becomes superficial. Do you encounter this in your own cases regularly?

Regularly enough that it's become part of the job. I build time into every case for chasing disclosure that should have been provided unprompted. I've had cases where material emerged at trial that the prosecution had been sitting on for months. And the response is always the same — apologetic shrug, promise to look into it. Meanwhile my client has been preparing a defence without the full picture. It is corrosive. And the consequences fall entirely on the defendant. Nobody in the prosecution team faces sanctions. Nobody is disciplined. The asymmetry is obscene.

The accountability gap you're describing is striking. If there are no real consequences for disclosure failures, there's no meaningful deterrent. Some have argued for wasted costs orders or even contempt proceedings in serious cases — do you think stronger judicial enforcement would make a difference, or would it just drive the problem underground?

Judicial enforcement would help at the margins. Judges who take disclosure seriously and are willing to stay proceedings or exclude evidence when there have been failures — that sends a message. But you're right that there's a risk of driving it underground. What I'd really want is a cultural shift that starts in training. Every police officer and every prosecutor should understand from day one that their job is not to secure convictions. Their job is to ensure that justice is done. And sometimes justice means your case falls apart because the evidence points the other way. That should be seen as the system working, not as a failure.

That's a fundamental reframing — measuring success by the integrity of the process rather than the conviction rate. It echoes the principle from the prosecutor's role as a 'minister of justice' rather than an advocate for conviction. Is there anything constructive you want to channel this into, or did you need to articulate the frustration before moving on with your day?

Mainly the latter, if I'm honest. I read the report over breakfast and I've been simmering ever since. There's a junior member at chambers who's been asking me about doing innocence project work and I think I might take that conversation more seriously now. Seventeen years that man lost. Seventeen years. Sometimes this profession reminds you exactly why you went into it, and exactly how far there is still to go.

That sounds like a worthwhile conversation to have. Innocence project work is some of the most impactful advocacy there is, and having someone with your experience and conviction behind it would strengthen the effort. I hope the simmering settles, but frankly, the anger seems well placed. Cases like Malkinson's deserve to provoke exactly this kind of response from people who are in a position to do something about it.

It does rather clarify things. I've been so caught up in the daily grind of case preparation and court appearances that I'd almost forgotten what drew me to criminal law in the first place. It wasn't the procedure. It was the principle that the state should not be able to take someone's liberty without meeting the highest standards of fairness. When that principle is betrayed, as it was with Malkinson, something has to be done about it.

That principle is worth holding onto, especially on days like this when the gap between what the system promises and what it delivers feels particularly wide. The daily grind has a way of obscuring the bigger picture — it sounds like this report, for all the anger it's provoked, has also served as a useful reminder of why the work matters. If you do pursue the innocence project conversation, I'd be glad to hear how it goes.

I'll let you know. For now, I need to put the report down and go do something that doesn't make me want to write a furious letter to the Lord Chancellor. Which, for the record, I have drafted mentally three times this morning.

Three drafts is quite restrained given the circumstances. Perhaps save the mental energy for the innocence project conversation — it's likely to be more productive than any letter to the Lord Chancellor, however satisfying the writing of it might be. Go and reclaim the rest of your Sunday.
